section.title: The Oracle against Egypt
section.sectionRationale: This opens the oracles against the nations, chapters 46 through 51, the long gallery that closes the body of Jeremiah before its historical appendix. Egypt stands first because Egypt was Judah's oldest and most tempting refuge, the power kings kept turning to instead of trusting the LORD. Here the LORD of Hosts pronounces sentence on Pharaoh's army, then on Pharaoh's own land and gods, and closes, remarkably, with tenderness for Jacob.
section.thesis: The LORD of Hosts, not Pharaoh or the gods of Egypt, governs the rise and fall of every earthly power; the same word that judges Egypt to the ground promises Jacob discipline without destruction.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the word concerning Egypt at Carchemish; Egypt's rout mocked in advance; Nebuchadnezzar's coming invasion of Egypt itself; judgment on Pharaoh and Egypt's gods, with mercy after; and the LORD's tender word to Jacob, do not be afraid.

units.label: The word concerning Egypt at Carchemish
units.note: 605 BC, the battle that made Babylon supreme

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD about the nations, the word that came to Jeremiah the prophet

concerning Egypt and the army of Pharaoh Neco king of Egypt, which was defeated at Carchemish on the Euphrates River by Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah king of Judah:

units.label: Egypt's rout mocked in advance
units.note: the day of vengeance no army can escape

verses.text: “Deploy your shields, small and large; advance for battle!
Harness the horses; mount the steeds; take your positions with helmets on! Polish your spears; put on armor!
Why am I seeing this? They are terrified, they are retreating; their warriors are defeated, they flee in haste without looking back; terror is on every side!” declares the LORD.
“The swift cannot flee, and the warrior cannot escape! In the north by the River Euphrates they stumble and fall.
Who is this, rising like the Nile, like rivers whose waters churn?
Egypt rises like the Nile, and its waters churn like rivers, boasting, ‘I will rise and cover the earth; I will destroy the cities and their people.’
Advance, O horses! Race furiously, O chariots! Let the warriors come forth: Cush and Put carrying their shields, men of Lydia drawing the bow.

For that day belongs to the Lord GOD of Hosts, a day of vengeance against His foes. The {{chereb|sword}} will devour until it is satisfied, until it is quenched with their blood. For the Lord GOD of Hosts will hold a sacrifice in the land of the north by the River Euphrates.
Go up to Gilead for balm, O Virgin Daughter of Egypt! In vain you try many remedies, but for you there is no healing.
The nations have heard of your shame, and your outcry fills the earth, because warrior stumbles over warrior and both of them have fallen together.”
This is the word that the LORD spoke to Jeremiah the prophet about the coming of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon to strike the land of Egypt:
“Announce it in Egypt, and proclaim it in Migdol; proclaim it in Memphis and Tahpanhes: ‘Take your positions and prepare yourself, for the sword devours those around you.’
Why have your warriors been laid low? They cannot stand, for the LORD has thrust them down.
They continue to stumble; indeed, they have fallen over one another. They say, ‘Get up! Let us return to our people and to the land of our birth, away from the sword of the oppressor.’
There they will cry out: ‘Pharaoh king of Egypt was all noise; he has let the appointed time pass him by.’
As surely as I live, declares the King, whose name is the LORD of Hosts, there will come one who is like Tabor among the mountains and like Carmel by the sea.
Pack your bags for exile, O daughter dwelling in Egypt! For Memphis will be laid waste, destroyed and uninhabited.
Egypt is a beautiful heifer, but a gadfly from the north is coming against her.

Even the mercenaries among her are like fattened calves. They too will turn back; together they will flee, they will not stand their ground, for the day of calamity is coming upon them, the time of their punishment.
Egypt will hiss like a fleeing serpent, for the enemy will advance in force; with axes they will come against her like woodsmen cutting down trees.
They will chop down her forest, declares the LORD, dense though it may be, for they are more numerous than locusts; they cannot be counted.
The Daughter of Egypt will be put to shame; she will be delivered into the hands of the people of the north.”

units.label: Judgment on Pharaoh and Egypt's gods, with mercy after
units.note: Amon of Thebes named for punishment

verses.text: The LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: “Behold, I am about to punish {{amon|Amon}} god of Thebes, along with Pharaoh, Egypt with her gods and kings, and those who trust in Pharaoh.
I will deliver them into the hands of those who seek their lives, of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and his officers. But after this, Egypt will be inhabited as in days of old, declares the LORD.

But you, O Jacob My servant, do not be afraid, and do not be dismayed, O Israel. For I will surely save you out of a distant place, your descendants from the land of their captivity! Jacob will return to quiet and ease, with no one to make him afraid.

And you, My servant Jacob, do not be afraid, declares the LORD, for I am with you. Though I will completely destroy all the nations to which I have banished you, I will not completely destroy you. Yet I will discipline you justly, and will by no means leave you unpunished.”

chereb.gloss: Sword. The recurring image across Jeremiah's oracles against the nations: the LORD wields history like a blade, and Egypt's boasted army cannot escape it, verse 10, until He is satisfied that justice has been done.

amon.gloss: Amon, chief god of the Egyptian pantheon, worshiped at Thebes as king of the gods. That the LORD names him for punishment, alongside Pharaoh and all Egypt's gods and kings, declares that Israel's God alone reigns over every throne and shrine, however ancient or exalted.

basic.context: Jeremiah 46 opens the oracles against the nations, chapters 46 through 51, the long collection that closes the body of the book before its historical appendix. It falls in two dated oracles: first a taunting picture of Pharaoh Neco's army routed at Carchemish in 605 BC, the battle that made Babylon the new master of the ancient Near East, and then a further word of Nebuchadnezzar's coming invasion of Egypt itself. Judgment on the mightiest of Judah's neighbors closes, remarkably, with a tender word to Jacob: do not be afraid.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical oracle, substantially fulfilled: Carchemish did break Egypt's power in 605 BC, and Nebuchadnezzar did later campaign against Egypt. It describes God's actual dealings with a particular empire; it is not a coded prediction about modern nations. The abiding truth is that the LORD of Hosts governs the rise and fall of every earthly power, and disciplines His own people in a different key than He judges their enemies.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on the chereb, the sword of the LORD, that devours until it is satisfied. In the middle of Egypt's roll call of gods stands Amon of Thebes, chief of the Egyptian pantheon, whom the LORD names for judgment alongside Pharaoh himself, a direct claim that Israel's God outranks every god Egypt could set against Him.
basic.typology: The sword of the LORD that no one can escape looks forward to the final judgment entrusted to Christ, who alone wields the sword of ultimate justice, Revelation 19:15. Egypt's gods, judged and shamed alongside Pharaoh, anticipate the greater unmasking of every false power accomplished at the cross, Colossians 2:15.
basic.christ: The LORD of Hosts holds a day appointed for vengeance against every proud power, and no army, however boastful, can hide from it, verse 10. That day finds its full and final shape in Christ, who comes as the rider on the white horse to strike down the nations and tread the winepress of the wrath of God Almighty, Revelation 19:11 to 15. Yet this same chapter that judges Egypt closes with grace for Jacob: do not be afraid, for I am with you, verse 28, a promise made certain in the One who is Immanuel, God with us.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the greatest empires: Pharaoh's army, gods, and boasting all fall exactly as He speaks. Yet He disciplines His own people differently from how He judges their oppressors: Jacob will be justly disciplined but never destroyed, verse 28, and even Egypt, after judgment, is promised to be inhabited again as in days of old, verse 26, a stray beam of the mercy that runs through every one of these oracles.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 24:7 records that after Carchemish the king of Egypt did not venture out of his land again. Ezekiel 29 gives a parallel oracle against Egypt and Pharaoh. Revelation 19:11 to 15 shows Christ wielding the final sword of judgment. Colossians 2:15 shows Christ disarming every ruler and authority.
basic.oneStory: This oracle shows that the LORD of Hosts, not Pharaoh or any god of Egypt, governs the nations and their days of reckoning. The sword that no one can escape belongs finally to Christ, who judges every proud power at His return; yet the same chapter that judges the nations preserves a note of mercy for Jacob, a pattern completed in the gospel that gathers a people for God from every nation He has judged.

context.literary: The first and longest panel in the oracles against the nations, chapters 46 to 51, itself in two dated sub-oracles: verses 2 to 12 look back at the rout of Carchemish, and verses 13 to 26 look ahead to Nebuchadnezzar's invasion of Egypt itself. The tender closing word to Jacob, verses 27 to 28, nearly repeats Jeremiah 30:10 to 11, tying this whole collection back to the Book of Consolation.
context.historical: Pharaoh Neco marched north in 609 BC and again met Babylon's crown prince Nebuchadnezzar at Carchemish on the Euphrates in 605 BC, where Egypt's army was decisively broken, ending Egyptian ambitions in the Levant and clearing Babylon's path to become the dominant empire, the very power Judah would soon answer to.
context.canonical: Part of the wider collection of oracles against the nations found also in Isaiah 13 to 23, Ezekiel 25 to 32, and Amos 1 to 2, showing that the LORD's moral government extends over every nation, whether Judah trusted it as an ally or feared it as an enemy.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare that the LORD of Hosts, not Pharaoh's army or Egypt's gods, decides the fate of nations, warning Judah against ever again leaning on Egypt for rescue, while still promising Jacob discipline rather than destruction.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment on Egypt; it is not prescribing hostility toward Egypt or any modern nation. The abiding principle is that no earthly power, however ancient or mighty, can shield those who trust in it instead of the LORD.

debates.question: Why does an oracle of judgment on Egypt end with comfort for Jacob, verses 27 to 28?

views.label: A deliberate contrast built into the whole collection
views.case: Each oracle against the nations ends in real desolation, yet Israel's own coming exile is framed differently throughout Jeremiah: disciplined, but preserved, so the contrast teaches Judah what kind of judgment to expect for herself.

views.label: An editorial seam echoing Jeremiah 30
views.case: Verses 27 to 28 closely repeat Jeremiah 30:10 to 11, suggesting this promise was placed here deliberately to frame the entire oracles against the nations with the assurance that Israel's future differs from every enemy she watches fall.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage teaches that God's severity toward the nations and God's discipline of His own covenant people are not the same thing; judgment on Egypt underlines, by contrast, the mercy reserved for Jacob.

study.originalLanguages: The chereb, the sword of the LORD, runs through the chapter as the instrument of a day appointed for vengeance, verse 10, an image of history itself as a blade no army can turn aside. At the heart of the judgment stands Amon, the god of Thebes, named for punishment beside Pharaoh, a direct challenge to the whole pantheon Egypt trusted.

typology.type: The sword of the LORD that devours until satisfied
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who wields the sword of final judgment at His return, Revelation 19:15.

typology.type: The gods of Egypt judged alongside Pharaoh
typology.fulfillment: Every ruler and authority disarmed and shamed at the cross, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: The LORD of Hosts holds a day appointed for vengeance against every proud power, and no army, however boastful, can hide from it, verse 10. That day finds its full and final shape in Christ, who comes as the rider on the white horse to strike down the nations and tread the winepress of the wrath of God Almighty, Revelation 19:11 to 15. Yet this same chapter that judges Egypt closes with grace for Jacob: do not be afraid, for I am with you, verse 28, a promise made certain in the One who is Immanuel, God with us.
study.god: God is sovereign over the greatest empires: Pharaoh's army, gods, and boasting all fall exactly as He speaks. Yet He disciplines His own people differently from how He judges their oppressors: Jacob will be justly disciplined but never destroyed, verse 28, and even Egypt, after judgment, is promised to be inhabited again as in days of old, verse 26, a stray beam of the mercy that runs through every one of these oracles.

crossReferences.note: Confirms that after Carchemish the king of Egypt never again ventured beyond his own land, the historical fulfillment of this oracle.
A parallel oracle against Egypt and Pharaoh, showing the same divine verdict announced through another prophet.
Christ appears with a sword to strike the nations, the final form of the day of vengeance announced in verse 10.
Christ disarms every ruler and authority, the ultimate unmasking of every god Egypt or any nation could trust.

study.oneStory: This oracle shows that the LORD of Hosts, not Pharaoh or any god of Egypt, governs the nations and their days of reckoning. The sword that no one can escape belongs finally to Christ, who judges every proud power at His return; yet the same chapter that judges the nations preserves a note of mercy for Jacob, a pattern completed in the gospel that gathers a people for God from every nation He has judged.

quiz.q: What historical battle stands behind the first oracle in Jeremiah 46?

options.text: The battle of Carchemish in 605 BC, where Nebuchadnezzar defeated Pharaoh Neco and Babylon rose to supremacy.
options.note: Verse 2 dates the oracle to this decisive battle on the Euphrates.

options.text: The fall of Nineveh to the Babylonians.
options.note: A real and related event in the same era, but not the battle this oracle names.

options.text: The exodus from Egypt under Moses.
options.note: An earlier and unrelated episode in Israel's history.

options.text: A naval battle between Egypt and the Philistines.
options.note: No such battle is described in this passage.

quiz.q: What does the LORD name for judgment alongside Pharaoh in verse 25?

options.text: Amon, the god of Thebes, together with Egypt's other gods and kings.
options.note: Verse 25 explicitly names Amon of Thebes for punishment, declaring the LORD's supremacy over Egypt's whole pantheon.

options.text: The Nile River itself, worshiped as a god.
options.note: Close to the theme of false worship in this chapter, but the text specifically names Amon of Thebes, not the Nile.

options.text: The king of Babylon.
options.note: Nebuchadnezzar is the LORD's instrument of judgment here, not its object.

options.text: No one; the oracle only concerns Pharaoh's army.
options.note: Verse 25 explicitly widens the judgment to Egypt's gods and kings.

quiz.q: What note of mercy appears even in this oracle of judgment?

options.text: The LORD promises Egypt will be inhabited again as in days of old, and Jacob is told not to fear.
options.note: Verses 26 to 28 fold mercy into judgment for both Egypt and Israel.

options.text: Egypt is promised total exemption from any further judgment.
options.note: This overstates it; Egypt is still judged, but restoration is promised afterward.

options.text: Pharaoh is allowed to keep his throne.
options.note: Pharaoh's power failing is precisely the point of this oracle.

options.text: There is no mercy anywhere in this chapter.
options.note: The chapter closes with real notes of hope for both Egypt and for Jacob.

quiz.q: How does the LORD treat Israel differently from Egypt in this chapter?

options.text: He promises to discipline Jacob justly but never to destroy him completely, unlike the nations.
options.note: Verse 28 states this distinction directly.

options.text: He promises Israel will never again be disciplined.
options.note: The text promises real discipline for Israel, just never destruction.

options.text: He treats Israel exactly the same as every other nation.
options.note: Verses 27 to 28 draw a clear distinction from the fate of the nations.

options.text: He abandons Israel to the same fate as Egypt.
options.note: The opposite is promised: Jacob will return to quiet and ease.

quiz.q: How does the sword of the LORD in this chapter point toward Christ?

options.text: It anticipates the final judgment Christ executes at His return, pictured with a sword in Revelation 19.
options.note: Revelation 19:11 to 15 pictures Christ wielding final judgment, the fulfillment of this day of vengeance.

options.text: It shows that swords are always symbols of evil in Scripture.
options.note: Swords can also represent just judgment, as here and in Revelation 19; the image is not uniformly negative.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: Scripture draws a direct line from this day of vengeance to Christ's final judgment.

options.text: It proves God is powerless against human armies.
options.note: The opposite is true: the sword devours precisely because the LORD Himself wields it.

questions: Egypt boasted, 'I will rise and cover the earth,' yet fell exactly as the LORD said. What strength or resource are you tempted to boast in in the same way?
Judah kept turning to Egypt for rescue instead of trusting the LORD. What is your 'Egypt', the reliable looking help you reach for before you pray?
The LORD named Amon of Thebes for judgment, refusing to let any god share His throne. What competing loyalties in your own heart need to be named honestly before God?
Even in judging Egypt, the LORD promised she would be inhabited again in days to come. Where do you need to trust that God's severity is not His last word?
Jacob was told not to fear because the LORD was with him, even under real discipline. How does knowing God disciplines in love, not to destroy, change how you receive correction?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints an em dash before 'Cush and Put', it has been rendered as a colon per house style, introducing the roster of allied warriors.
Poetic line breaks in verses such as 5, 16, and 17 have been joined into running verse text per house style.
Where BSB prints em dashes, they have been rendered as commas per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Jeremiah 46:1-28. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; em dashes rendered as commas or colons per house style.
